content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Andrew Morris 
Town Attorney 
Town of Truckee 
IO 1 83 Truckee Airport Road 
Truckee, CA 96161-3306 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-255 
Dear Mr. Morris: 
December 7, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section I 090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) l 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
I. May Best Best & Krieger ("BBK") continue handling the specific matters that the firm is 
currently engaged on for Truckee and Mammoth Lakes? 
2. May employees of Truckee and Mammoth Lakes (other than you) refer work to BBK if 
you do not have involvement in the selection of the firm to which work is referred and have 
procured the standing availability of attorneys other than BBK attorneys such that procuring special 
counsel services will not necessarily result in work going to BBK? 
3. May you suggest to staff that outside counsel be engaged for particular 
matters? 
4. If BBK is permitted to continue providing services to Truckee and/or Mammoth Lakes, 
may you discuss such work, strategize, coordinate, etc. with the BBK attorneys who are providing 
such services? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-17-255 
Page No. 2 
1-4. You will not have a conflict of interest if, pursuant to a contract made with either city 
prior to your participation in the decision, the city has agreed that it may retain the law firm to 
handle those matters. You may not make a recommendation to either city that will create additional 
work and income for your wife's employer that is beyond the scope of the contract under which her 
employer is rendering advice. 
FACTS 
You have served as town attorney for the Town of Mammoth Lakes since late 2010 and for 
the Town of Truckee since late 2012. Until January 1, 2014, you were a partner in the law firm 
BBK. Since then, you have been an employee of Truckee and represent Mammoth Lakes as a 
contract town attorney. 
When you were with BBK, various other attorneys within the firm provided specialized 
services to both Truckee and Mammoth Lakes on matters outside the scope of your expertise. This 
practice has continued to the present day, and both Truckee and Mammoth Lakes have open-ended 
legal services agreements in place with BBK. In some cases, you suggest to Truckee or Mammoth 
Lakes staff that they seek advice from BBK, and in other cases, staff members who have previously 
worked with BBK attorneys will contact those attorneys directly. Matters that BBK is currently 
handling for Truckee or Mammoth Lakes include an employee benefits dispute with CalPERS, 
eminent domain proceedings, code enforcement matters, employee discipline and general labor 
work, CEQA, and public contracting issues. 
Your wife, Stefanie Morris, is also an attorney. She was employed by BBK until late 2013 
and has since practiced elsewhere. She reached an agreement to return to BBK, and commenced 
employment with BBK on October 30, 2017. You note that Ms. Morris' return to the firm is 
m:rrelated to the work that BBK does for Truckee and Mammoth Lakes, and her practice (water 
rights) is unrelated to anything that BBK does for either city. In a telephone conversation on 
November 6, 2017, you noted that she is a partner with BBK, but has no equity in the firm. You 
also note that you have requested proposals to handle work for Truckee and Mammoth Lakes on an 
as-needed basis from another municipal law firm, with which you have no connection. That firm is 
similar to BBK in that it can provide attorneys with expertise in any area of municipal law, 
including all the pending matters that BBK is handling for both towns. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official 
has a financial interest. 
As pertinent to your facts, interests under the Act that are potentially disqualifying include 
any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received income of 
$500 or more within 12 months before the decision. A public official's community property interest 
in a spouse's income is considered income to the public official. (Section 82030, subdivision (a).) 


File No. A-17-255 
Page No. 3 
We have generally advised that, where a decision to pay the entity in which the official has 
an economic interest for additional work was contemplated by a contract previously made by 
disinterested agency officials, the official's participation merely constitutes the implementation of 
that preexisting decision. (McEwen Advice Letter No. 1-92-481.) 
If a contract, negotiated or bid upon by an attorney or law firm in a private capacity, under 
which the attorney provides services to the agency includes a provision that permits the agency to 
also retain the counsel or his or her law firm to handle litigation on which the counsel has been 
advising the agency, then there is no conflict of interest under the Act if the counsel or his or her 
firm receives additional compensation to do so. That is because the government decision to pay this 
compensation was already made by the agency when the consultant contract was negotiated or bid 
upon and not at the later time when the agency confers with the counsel on whether to pursue or 
defend the litigation. (Pansky Advice Letter, No. 1-14-096.) 
However, ifthere is no provision in the consultant contract under which the agency may 
also retain the attorney or his or her law firm to handle litigation on which the attorney advises the 
agency, then the attorney likely would have a conflict of interest under the Act if the attorney or his 
or her firm receives additional compensation for these services. 
You will not have a conflict as long as you do not make a recommendation to either city that 
will create additional work and income for your wife's employer that is beyond the scope of the 
contract under which her employer is rendering advice. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
Qh:n 
By: 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

such services?
